text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi, Emiliano.

I'm doing great.
And you?
I'm good.

Oh, amazing.
Buenos Aires.
I haven't been there since 2008, but...
That's a long time, man.
I know.
It's a long time.
Still feels like yesterday.

I mean, I loved it.
I studied abroad there.
Oh, cool.
So, yeah.
It's super sick
Yeah, I think it was there for four or five months.
It's doing better in some aspects and doing worse in some, but I think that overall it's a better city than the one you visited in 2008.
I have to go back.
You definitely do.
It's still a good place.
If you can, you know, it's like, it's a little bit like New York.
You know, you want to visit, but you don't want to stay a long, long time.
Yeah, does drive you a little crazy, but as long as you can, you know, just get out and come back, it's good.
All right.
Are you from there?
Have you lived there?
Probably my entire life, although on and off.
So, and I don't live in like, you know, the very city of Buenos Aires, kind of like the downtown area.
I live on the west side, which is, it's a different reality.
So, you know, you got the city and you got the north, west, south sides from the west side.
So, side of the the the
It's like, more like living in Brooklyn, right?
It's more, yeah, something like that.
Cool.
Awesome.
Well, thank you for scheduling time with us and being interested in this role.
I'm happy to give you a bit of a context about myself and how I'm thinking about the role to start.
And then we can jump into talking about you and I'll try and leave like five, 10 minutes at the end for you to ask me any questions you have.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Perfect.
So this role is, well, let's start with me.
I lead customer operations for GrowthX, which basically means I think about the process and pieces that go into all of the deliverables that we send out to clients.
And one of the things that we're really focused on right now internally is building our own platform that can leverage.
Leverage.
The AI systems, obviously, like, we touch a lot of AI systems to generate our content.

And so we build unified workflows eventually, and we're experimenting with agentic workflows that can build outlines, article briefs, do deep research, and generate, like, content at scale for our clients.
And, you know, we started by leveraging different tools to do that.
Now we're building that in-house.
And this role is really kind of two things at once.
So it's a bit of a project manager role, and in that capacity, you'll touch the platform itself.
You'll be working with the platform itself, but you'll be working within a pod of a content manager who will be actually running the workflows on a day-to-day basis, kind of pressing play.
You'll help.
help.
Troubleshoot, give feedback, and prioritize with that content manager.
And then you'll also be working under an account director who will think strategically about the account, set the direction, and come up with deliverables, analytics, things like that.

And this is, it's a little bit of that piece of working with the director on the client side is a client facing account manager a little bit, just in terms of like updates, that project management piece to the client.
So there's that technical piece of working with the platform and in the system.
And then there's the aspect of working with the director on making sure that the client is happy, they're getting what they need, and bringing that feedback back to your CM content manager.
So, um...
So that's the role.
Do you have any questions?
Yeah, but I could save them for later because it's what I would really like to get a clear mental picture of would be what a typical day would look like for me.
Yeah, yeah, I can.
Well, that's a good question.
So the managing editor we're expecting should be able to manage like three to five accounts on an ongoing basis.
And we talk to each of those accounts once a week and we have weekly deliverables for those clients.
So that usually means generating 10-ish pieces of content for them, making sure that they get published.
And within that kind of like pod, the content manager will put together the first draft using the workflows.

You'll be the editor thinking about brand, tone, refining that content, and ideally as the workflows get better, that work will be less and less, but we want that human in the loop and we want that tastemaker to kind of be touching each piece of content to make sure that it's aligning with what the client expects.
And then also helping pull analytics or anything else for client meetings.

So you'll probably be editing a couple of pieces of content every day, possibly helping generate if, you know, we've got a push or somebody's out of office, you might really, you might be helping the content manager.
And then preparing for weekly meetings with your clients.
Does that make sense?
That sounds really good.
Yeah, I was actually kind of thinking about what the whole workflow looked like, like who.
You know, where, for instance, where does a piece of content begin, for instance?
So, right.
Yeah.
Does it end with me?
Right.
Am I the final link in the chain before it gets delivered to the client?
Yeah.
Usually you will be.
Yes.
Right.
And so where does a piece of content begin?
Like, who generates it?
Who starts, quote, unquote, like writing it or refining it?
Is that the person that comes before me or is that, does that finalize with me?
It's more or less kind of trying to understand the process.
Yeah.
So the workflows, we start with a client by generating keywords that we want to optimize for, for their SEO.
The workflows, you can plug those in with the brand voice.
We generate a lot of artifacts on company context.
And then then-

From there, we're building out the workflows that are, you input like a keyword, the artifacts, and it will generate an outline, the AI will generate an outline, you approve that outline, run another workflow, and the AI will generate the content.

Okay, okay, that does clear it up a bit, because I was, for instance, in my previous role, I was directly overseeing human writers, as in human writers, writers who were interacting with AI, but they were, let's see, we had different LLMs that we used, mostly Claude and ChatGPT, but we had our briefs, had our outlines, we had the, you know, client tone, brand voice, et cetera, et cetera, everything was put into different workspaces, and

Basically, the writer generated a draft, which was, you know, we already had custom prompts, or if the writer didn't have really good prompts, I helped them generate better prompts, which was, you know, something that I became really good at.
And this, as you know, all of these roles, all these workflows are very new, you know, they're very few companies are leveraging LLMs to make content at scale.

Yeah, and my previous employer was doing just that, but the way that the workflow looked was basically, we had a person who made briefs, and I helped that person make briefs, then we had writers, and then we had editors.
I started as a writer, but as the company grew, and as it became more evident that I was a very, very, very fast writer, I could write my allotted 12 to 14 pieces of content.
And two or three days.
And they were, they were typically, sorry, manually, not manually.
We used LLMs, but we did have to substantially rewrite the pieces because we definitely wanted to avoid, you know, the common AI vices.

By the end of my tenure, we had a list that was like five pages long of like stuff that we, we, we didn't religiously avoid it, but we tended not to use because, you know, over time, you start spotting all the repetition and all the stuff that, that, that basically, and people start spotting that stuff too.
You know, they, they start realizing that AI, even though it looks like a magic pill, it's, it's not that magical at this point, at least.
So, so we definitely disincentivize.

Using some construction, some phrases, we can use, we, by the end, we were basically forbidden, forbidding M-dashes, which I wasn't, I wasn't super, like, I could take or leave that restriction, but, but, AI does tend to overuse them a lot to separate causes.

So, basically, I started as a writer, and I was, I was very fast, the way I worked became the gold standard across the company, because it was very efficient, it was, so, I became very fast at using LLMs, and, you know, researching, writing, and I became really good at spotting what needed to be rewritten, and what could be left as is.
So, basically, what you wanted was to get very good prompts that gave you passable first drafts, or first drafts that were very usable, that avoided most of the...
You know, kind of the typical AI slop mistakes, you know, the stuff that tells you like this is going to revolutionize your workflow.

And, you know, and once I became good at that and the company started scaling, my former boss said, hey, would you like to start teaching our junior editors how to basically do their job and how to minimize their edits and how to, you know, liaise with writers?
And then it evolved again to the point where I was overseeing two or three other writers every week alongside with writing my own content.
So, that was what I was doing by the end.
was basically writing or manually generating and rewriting 12 to 14 pieces of content weekly of SEO content.
And then...
I'm junior editors and editing content myself.
So it was pretty busy.
So what you're telling me sounds a little bit more relaxed, although from what you're telling me, I would have to be doing it with a variety of clients.
So that also means learning different tones, different brand voices, preferences, aligning feedback with the content that we're generating.
But it sounds very doable from what you're telling me.
It sounds very manageable.
And I don't mind the client-facing part of the job because I think Marilia told me that some people were expressing some concerns about, you know, meetings and whatever.
But from what she told me, it's not very intrusive.
It's not.
And I also don't mind, you know, like I tend or I think my pre
Expositions were asynchronous.
I think that's the way you say it.
But I don't mind being online for X hours of the day and, you know, writing to clients, taking meetings.
That's not something I'm buying.
Yeah.
So talk to me a little bit about your prioritization process across, like, if you are managing multiple clients.
If I imagine multiple clients, first of all, it depends on whether we have clients that generate more pieces or have been with us for longer.
But usually I tend to prioritize clients that are not as established in the sense that, for instance, if we have a new client, then we need to, there's a lot that we need to get down.
Like, we need to understand what good content looks for them.
We need to create.
From scratch, probably some brand guidelines that include their voice, their preference, their tone, their customers, create a knowledge base.
That's what I tend to do first.
So if we have clients that have very established knowledge bases and you can generate reliable content, then that's easier because it's more automated.

But when you have no clients and you basically have to – what gives me peace of mind is just getting everything down on writing and just maybe generating a very – as broad a knowledge base as I can make from whether – from pricing, tone, who's their audience, who they're – what type of people they're aiming their content at, what they want to get out of this content.

How do they want to grow to their making a glossary to, so basically it's, I can even send you later some knowledge bases that I've written for previous clients, but basically you want to get as much detail down as possible.
That's why I prioritize first.
I prioritize the content that has to be delivered that week.

I'm editing everything that the writers or the content managers have made before, making sure that it aligns with each and every guideline that we got for the client, and of course making sure that the client is happy first and foremost.
So I would be, you know, first you want to develop an ideal piece of content, basically.
You want to get everything about the client down, then you want to create an ideal piece of content, something that the client is.
Happy top to bottom with, and then you want to use that as a template to continue creating.
But that would be my priority, to just make a single piece of content that's perfect, and it's the gold standard for the client.
Yeah, what about prioritizing across different clients for different pieces of content?
You're managing five clients a week.
How do you start your week prioritizing across those clients?
Again, it depends on how much content we have to deliver for each client.
But basically, my Monday would be a very, very, very busy day, I assume.
So first of all, you'd probably start a day trying to juggle feedback from multiple clients.
And to probably get, so I was, again, you would have to remind me on what the, what, what the other two roles that I would be.
Ideally.
Yazing with are called, but because one is the, I think you said, it's not an account manager, but I would be a content manager.
like a director and a content manager.
But mean, just how do you prioritize your own time?
How do you think about that?
Again, it's very hard to get a clear picture until I'm in the trenches, but I could tell you how I prioritize my time before, as in with the roles that I've already had.
So first of all, what I always prioritized was the content that I had to write myself.

Then whether there was any feedback from clients or any conversations that needed to be had with clients about whether they were happy or not with content, whether we need to make any changes, any edits that were requested, those are always a priority.
Then...
It's creating the content that's been directly assigned to me, and then it's reviewing and editing the content that's been made by other writers or other people in the pipeline.
One thing that is different from this role is that it wasn't typical for me to be involved in client meetings, although it happened from time to time.

But I was always liaising with them directly on Slack, so I was always online, I was always talking directly to somebody who was responsible for overseeing content and making edits as fast as possible.
So edits and feedback were always my main focus, my main priority.
Got it.
Okay, well, have 10 minutes left.
What questions do you have for me?
I think that I've actually asked you the most important question for
I which was what a typical day would look like.
And maybe I think I asked this to Marilia, but she didn't really tell me what types of content we would be doing.
I think she mentioned SEO, programmatic content, but I don't know if we touch any other types of content.
Yeah, I mean, that's really what we focus on is SEO optimization, so editorial content for blogs and stuff like that.
Our clients range the gamut, so I think, like, topically, it could go anywhere.
And also, something else that I asked her was, but I think you also answered, was how many pieces of content I would be maybe reviewing, editing, et cetera, per week.
She said the typical week was, like, 10 to 12, but it could be more if we're, you know.
Making a big push on like programmatic SEO content or, but I thought that those numbers were extremely manageable.
Like I said, I was looking at probably 40 to 50 pieces per week.
So in my previous position and I think it might be pretty good in my opinion, but we already had a very, very good process going.
And I actually, like the good part about that is that I can bring a lot of what worked to this process too.
Because I've got, I got a, I got a million prompt stacks and tools already that can help me, you know, get a, get a very good workflow going quickly.
And let's see, I think Marilla, you also told me that you, you guys have been.
And, uh, uh,
The company's relatively young, right?
Like they're like 11 months old or something like that.
And you've been in this role for two months, right?
Yeah, just about.
Yeah.
So my role, is that new as well?
Is that kind of like a new?
No, we have about 15 of this role.
Okay, great.
Really fast.
Right.

So, again, what would a pod look like?
Yeah, I covered that at the beginning.
It's a CM, you, and a director.
That's a director.
And each of those, each pod works with three to five clients?
Yeah.
Uh, yeah.
Great.
And the directors have multiple pods.
The directors have multiple pods.
And, um, yeah, no, I think that's, that's pretty much it for me.
So, uh, I don't think I got any other questions.

So that's, uh, I think that's pretty clear at this point, but I think that given my, um, given my previous experience editing and working with, with writers and clients, it looks like a very manageable workflow.
So again, the good thing is that, uh, even if the workflow spiked suddenly to like two to three times what you told me, um, I think it would still be manageable for me.
Considering my previous position.
That's great.
Yeah.

Because you said that maybe I would have to provide updates and metrics, et cetera.
Or would I be in charge of maybe leading those meetings or?
Sometimes.
Sometimes.
I don't think that's a problem either.
And typically we're talking about one to two client meetings per week, right, to update them how the content is doing.
One.
So five client meetings per week, one per client.
That's, yeah, that's no problem at all.
I, because Marilia, you know, when she, she was kind of dreading asking that question to me at first, because she said that a
lot of people kind of like basically noked out of the process when she told them that they would be in charge of client meetings, and I don't really have a problem with that.
I actually love talking about clients.
Great.
Yeah.

Yeah, perfect.
I will.
Sorry.

Yeah, no problem at all.

was great to meet you.
